text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr A. Rahman  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr A. Rahman,  

Alex Morgan, DOB: 21-07-1991, Hospital No: U842915 NHS No. 1039485726  
Flat 3, 12 Ironworks Lane, London, EC1A 7LP 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Focal epilepsy with impaired awareness originating from the left temporal region/possible heat and sleep-deprivation provocation in commercial kitchen setting/generalised anxiety symptoms related to work environment/gastro-oesophageal reflux disease/smoker.  

Medication: Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily.  
Brivaracetam 100 mg twice daily.  
Omeprazole 20 mg once daily.  
As‑needed salbutamol inhaler for occasional wheeze (patient-reported).  

I reviewed Alex in clinic today. He works as a line cook in a busy commercial kitchen with sustained high ambient temperatures and rapid task turnover. He reports that service periods are intense and noisy, with frequent transitions between fryers, grill, and pass. He notes that bright heat lamps and long shifts contribute to fatigue and dehydration; he now carries a water bottle at the station and rotates briefly to prep when possible, as agreed informally with his head chef. 

Seizures: He has focal epilepsy. He describes stereotyped focal impaired-awareness episodes beginning with a rising epigastric sensation and metallic taste, followed by behavioural arrest, lip-smacking, and right-hand fumbling, typically lasting 60–90 seconds with brief post-event confusion. He remains standing in most episodes but may pause at the grill or pass until a colleague redirects him. He reports events occur daily, most commonly during the late dinner rush between 19:00 and 21:00, particularly on days after poor sleep. He denies generalised tonic–clonic seizures. No clusters, injuries, or burns this month; colleagues recognise the semiology and guide him to a safe area when possible. Last witnessed event by manager was three days ago; Alex logged similar events on his phone diary daily over the past four weeks. No clear missed-dose association; he states adherence is good and uses a pill alarm. 

Examination: Alert, oriented, normal speech and language. No focal motor deficit. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations unremarkable. No tongue trauma or limb bruising. 

Investigations: Prior MRI head (2023) showed left mesial temporal sclerosis. Prior EEG (2023) with left temporal interictal discharges. Routine bloods (Aug 2025): FBC, U&E, LFTs within reference ranges; lamotrigine level within therapeutic range (patient brought copy). No recent brivaracetam level available. 

Occupational context: Patient reports high-heat exposure from grill and fryers, prolonged standing, and limited breaks. He avoids carrying heavy pots of hot oil and has agreed with the employer to step back from the fry station during peak heat; this is employer-reported to be workable on most shifts. He is comfortable wearing cut- and heat-resistant gloves; he has begun using a timer to prompt short hydration breaks. 

Plan: 
- Continue lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily and brivaracetam 100 mg twice daily for now. 
- Arrange epilepsy nurse review to reinforce self-management around identified triggers (sleep deprivation, heat, dehydration) and to optimise a workplace seizure response plan with the employer. 
- Request trough brivaracetam level and repeat U&E/LFTs in the next 2–4 weeks via GP. 
- Provide written seizure first-aid guidance tailored to the kitchen environment and confirm safe task allocation during peak heat periods. 
- Consider add-on perampanel if daily focal impaired-awareness events persist despite adherence and trigger mitigation; to be discussed at next review after results. 
- Follow-up in 3 months or sooner if event pattern worsens. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: He reports events occur daily, most commonly during the late dinner rush between 19:00 and 21:00

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states that the patient reports events occur daily and has logged similar events daily over the past four weeks. There is no mention of seizure-free periods; thus, the current highest frequency is daily. Convert 'daily' to the allowed format as '1 per day.'
seizure_frequency_number: He reports events occur daily, most commonly during the late dinner rush between 19:00 and 21:00
